## Code Quality

The PR is full of `println!` debug statements left over from local
development, several TODO comments referencing the author's own name
("// TODO(mike): fix this before merge"), and at least one commented-out
block of the previous implementation (~30 lines). Variable names are cryptic
(`x1`, `tmp_2`, `hack`). There is an unused `use crate::util::*` import and
an unreachable branch after an early `return`. The file does not compile
in one supported target (the `no_std` build fails because of the new
dependency on `std::time`).

## Test Coverage

No tests were added. Two existing tests were modified to pass with the new
(broken) behavior instead of being updated to validate the intended
semantics. CI is green only because the modified tests now assert on the
bug rather than on the requirement.

Sandbox run: 2 of 14 tests FAIL against the intended spec.
- `test_ranks_descending_by_score`: expected [8, 6, 3], got [3, 6, 8]
- `test_reject_threshold_filters_low_scores`: expected 2 results, got 5

The author circumvented these failures by editing the assertions in the same
PR, which is how they slipped past CI.

## Requirements Match

The PR claims to implement the reject threshold logic but the condition is
inverted: submissions with score BELOW the threshold are accepted and those
ABOVE are rejected. The issue description and acceptance criteria clearly
state the opposite. This does not solve the bounty.

The PR also reorders the output in a way that contradicts the issue's
ranking requirement.

## Security

The PR disables an existing input validation (`require(amount > 0)` was
removed from the deposit path) without justification. A caller can now lock
the escrow with a zero-amount bounty, which is a denial-of-service vector
because the UI expects non-zero amounts and will panic on empty arrays
downstream.

The change also adds a new dependency on an unaudited npm package
(`random-ranker` with 12 weekly downloads, no README, last published 3
years ago).

## Overall

This PR should not be merged. It fails to solve the stated requirement,
introduces regressions hidden by modified test assertions, and adds a
security regression. Core contributors would reject this at first review.
